name: Harry Houdini
knownFor: The world's greatest escape artist and illusionist, who challenged supernatural claims and befriended — then publicly feuded with — Arthur Conan Doyle over spiritualism.
quickFacts: Born Erik Weisz in Budapest, 1874; died October 31, 1926 — Halloween
Could escape from handcuffs, straitjackets, locked trunks, and submerged crates
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 231) as simply 'Houdini — New York City'
His brother Theo Hardeen also visited the Aladdin (pg. 174) and signed 'Bro. of Harry Houdini'
A clipping was later tucked into the guestbook: 'Miss Mooser — You may want to see Houdini Film' (the 1953 Tony Curtis film)
Fierce debunker of fraudulent mediums and spirit seances

name: Theo Hardeen
knownFor: Harry Houdini's younger brother and fellow escape artist — who signed the Aladdin guestbook identifying himself as 'Bro. of Harry Houdini,' making this a direct family connection to the world's greatest magician.
quickFacts: Born Ehrich Weiss Jr. in Budapest, 1876 — two years after his famous brother
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 174) on May 17, 1927 while at the Orpheum Theatre
His wife Gladys Hardeen also signed, along with their Orpheum colleagues
Performed a similar escape act to his brother's and was quite successful in his own right
When Houdini died in 1926, Theo inherited his brother's props and secrets
He and Harry had a complex relationship — rivals and partners, brothers always

name: Rudolph Valentino
knownFor: The screen's first great romantic idol — 'The Sheik' — who made women swoon across America and whose death in 1926 triggered a mass public hysteria.
quickFacts: Born Rodolfo Alfonso Raffaello Pierre Filibert Guglielmi di Valentina d'Antonguolla in Italy, 1895
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 263) with the remark 'one of the 4 Horsemen'
His wife Jean Acker Valentino also signed the same page: 'To thine own self be true'
Famous for The Sheik (1921), Blood and Sand (1922), The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1921)
Died August 23, 1926 of pleuritis — 100,000 people lined the streets of New York for his funeral
Hattie and Minnie hosted him before his death — a true coup for the Aladdin

name: Jean Acker Valentino
knownFor: Rudolph Valentino's first wife — a silent film actress who signed the Aladdin guestbook on the same page as Valentino with the remark 'To thine own self be true.'
quickFacts: Born in Trenton, New Jersey, 1893
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 263) alongside Rudolph Valentino
Married Valentino in 1919 but locked him out of their hotel room on their wedding night — they separated immediately
Officially divorced in 1922, just before Valentino married Natacha Rambova
Was at the Aladdin at the same time as Valentino — their visit captures a complicated moment
Continued acting in films through the 1920s and 1930s

name: Jack Benny
knownFor: The master of the comic pause, whose radio and television persona as the world's cheapest man made him one of America's best-loved entertainers for five decades.
quickFacts: Born Benjamin Kubelsky in Chicago, 1894; grew up in Waukegan, Illinois
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 233 (Lake Forest, Ill.) and pg. 250 (Orpheum Circuit)
Was a vaudeville violinist before becoming a comedian
His radio show debuted 1932 and ran until 1955; TV show ran until 1965
Famous for his running gag of being 39 years old forever
George Burns was his lifelong best friend

name: Norma Shearer
knownFor: The Queen of MGM — the studio's reigning dramatic star of the late silent and early sound eras, who won the Academy Award for The Divorcee (1930).
quickFacts: Born in Montreal, Canada, 1902; came to Hollywood as a teenager
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 303) — her mother Edith Shearer also signed
Married MGM production chief Irving Thalberg in 1927
Won the Academy Award for Best Actress for The Divorcee (1930)
Known for playing sophisticated, sexually liberated women in pre-Code Hollywood
Retired from films in 1942 after Thalberg's death

name: Lon Chaney
knownFor: The Man of a Thousand Faces — Hollywood's supreme master of disguise and transformation, who created unforgettable monsters from the Hunchback of Notre Dame to the Phantom of the Opera.
quickFacts: Born Leonidas Frank Chaney in Colorado Springs, 1883, to deaf-mute parents
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 296) as 'Lon Chaney — Hollywood, Cal.'
Created his own makeup — often painful, always astounding — for each role
The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1923) and Phantom of the Opera (1925) are his masterpieces
Died of lung cancer in 1930, just as talking pictures arrived
His son Lon Chaney Jr. went on to play The Wolf Man

name: Sophie Tucker
knownFor: The Last of the Red Hot Mamas — a brassy, bawdy vaudeville superstar from Hartford whose big voice and bigger personality made her one of the longest-running acts in show business.
quickFacts: Born Sophia Kalish in Russia, 1887; raised in Hartford, Connecticut
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 243 (April 25, 1923) and pg. 362
Started as a blackface performer before shedding the makeup and becoming herself
Her signature song 'Some of These Days' became one of the most recorded songs of the era
Performed professionally for over 60 years
Called herself 'the last of the red hot mamas' — a term she invented

name: Leo Carrillo
knownFor: A beloved character actor descended from one of California's oldest Spanish land-grant families, who was so fond of the Aladdin he signed the guestbook twice and drew his own cartoon caricature both times.
quickFacts: Born in Los Angeles, 1881, to one of California's most prominent Californio families
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 135 ('Leo Carrillo '22 S.F.') and pg. 392 (1925)
On pg. 135 he drew a cartoon Chinese cook at a wok labeled 'LADDIM EAT EM WELLY GOODIE'
On pg. 392 he drew a self-caricature in the margin and signed it 'Leo Carrillo 1925'
Later famous as Pancho in The Cisco Kid TV series (1950-1956)
California's Leo Carrillo State Park is named in his honor

name: Larry Fine
knownFor: The middle Stooge — the frizzy-haired, long-suffering Larry Fine of the Three Stooges, who signed the Aladdin guestbook alongside his wife while performing at the Curran Theatre.
quickFacts: Born Louis Feinberg in Philadelphia, 1902
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 140) at the Curran Theatre — 'Mrs. Larry Fine' also signed
'Three Stooges' is written in the margin next to their entry
The Stooges were in 'A Night in Spain' at the Curran Theatre when they visited
Larry, Moe Howard, and Curly Howard made 190 short films for Columbia Pictures (1934-1959)
His violin skills were real — he trained seriously before becoming a comedian

name: Billy Frawley
knownFor: Best remembered as the curmudgeonly Fred Mertz on I Love Lucy — but in the 1920s he was a vaudeville song-and-dance man with a direct San Francisco connection: he performed a comedy act there called 'A Man, a Piano, and a Nut.'
quickFacts: Born in Burlington, Iowa, 1887; died 1966
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 143) with the dry remark 'liked it'
His San Francisco vaudeville act, 'Frawley and Louise,' performed regularly in the city with his then-wife
He and his wife divorced in 1927 — the same year he visited the Aladdin
Appeared in over 100 films before being cast as Fred Mertz on I Love Lucy in 1951
Was notorious for his love of baseball, beer, and blunt opinions — all in equal measure

name: Jane Darwell
knownFor: The actress who won the Academy Award for playing Ma Joad in The Grapes of Wrath (1940) — but who was already a busy Orpheum Circuit performer when she signed the Aladdin guestbook in the early 1920s.
quickFacts: Born Patti Woodward in Palmyra, Missouri, 1879; died 1967
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 220) with a checkmark — Hattie or Minnie marked her as notable
Her entry lists 'Nancy Co.' — she was touring in 'Nancy' when she visited
Won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for The Grapes of Wrath (1940) as the iconic Ma Joad
Appeared in over 170 films throughout her career, frequently playing maternal characters
Appeared in five Shirley Temple films and was still working into the 1960s

name: Merna Kennedy
knownFor: Charlie Chaplin's co-star as the circus bareback rider in The Circus (1928) — who signed the Aladdin guestbook on September 4, 1928 with 'The Circus' listed next to her name, on the exact same page as Roy Rogers.
quickFacts: Born Maude Kahler in Kankakee, Illinois, 1908; died 1944 at only 36
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 132) on September 4, 1928 — listed as from 'The Circus'
She was on the same page as Roy Rogers ('Anywhere'), Chaz Chase (Warfield), and Clara Kimball Young
Grew up on the Orpheum and Pantages vaudeville circuits — a child performer
It was her childhood friendship with Lita Grey that led to her introduction to Charlie Chaplin
Retired from acting in 1934 upon marrying choreographer Busby Berkeley

name: Chaz Chase
knownFor: The vaudeville eccentric who built an entire act around eating things that weren't food — lit cigarettes, paper flowers, cardboard, his own clothes — and kept audiences laughing for six decades.
quickFacts: Born Max Oskye in Kiev, 1901; died 1983
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 132) at the Warfield Theatre on September 4, 1928
Was on the same page as Roy Rogers, Merna Kennedy (The Circus), and Clara Kimball Young
Originally trained as a ballet dancer — inspired to become a comedian by watching Joe Frisco
His act: he appeared to eat lit matches, cigarettes, paper, cloth, and anything within reach
Had a career resurgence in the TV era, appearing on Ed Sullivan, Johnny Carson, and David Letterman

name: The Duncan Sisters
knownFor: The most famous sister act in vaudeville — Rosetta (the foghorn-voiced comic) and Vivian (the pretty blonde) — who performed their 'Topsy and Eva' musical comedy for decades and signed the Aladdin guestbook on several consecutive pages.
quickFacts: Rosetta (1894-1959) and Vivian (1897-1986) Duncan — sisters from Los Angeles
Signed the Aladdin guestbook across pages 232-236 at the Orpheum Theatre
Their act 'Topsy and Eva' was a musical comedy adaptation of Uncle Tom's Cabin — Rosetta played Topsy
MGM cast them in It's a Great Life (1929), one of the early Technicolor sound musicals
Rosetta died in 1959 from injuries after falling asleep at the wheel returning from a performance
Vivian lived until 1986 — she outlived her sister by 27 years

name: Richard Bennett
knownFor: A major Broadway and film star of the early 20th century, and father of three famous daughters — Constance, Joan, and Barbara Bennett — who signed the Aladdin guestbook twice in 1927.
quickFacts: Born in Cass County, Indiana, 1870; died 1944
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 177 and pg. 179 in 1927
Father of actresses Constance Bennett, Joan Bennett, and Barbara Bennett — three of Hollywood's most famous sisters
Famous for George Bernard Shaw's Man and Superman on Broadway (1905)
Played Major Amberson in Orson Welles's The Magnificent Ambersons (1942) late in his career
Met his third wife while producing a play called Creoles in San Francisco in 1925 — two years before his Aladdin visits

name: Garland Anderson
knownFor: The first African American to have a full-length drama produced on Broadway — 'Appearances' (1925) — who signed the Aladdin guestbook as 'Author of Appearances' and had deep roots in San Francisco going back to before the 1906 earthquake.
quickFacts: Born in Wichita, Kansas, 1886; died 1939
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 148) on March 19, 1928 as 'Garland Anderson, Author of Appearances'
His play Appearances (1925) was the first full-length drama by a Black playwright produced on Broadway
Had deep San Francisco roots — worked as a newsboy and bellhop there, predating the 1906 earthquake
Al Jolson financially supported Anderson's move to New York to get his play produced
His integrated Broadway cast was historically groundbreaking for American theater

name: Irene Franklin
knownFor: The fiery red-headed queen of vaudeville character comedy — voted 'Most Popular Woman Vaudeville Artist' in a 1908 industry poll — who began performing as an infant and never really stopped.
quickFacts: Born in New York City, 1885; died 1941
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 251) with a checkmark at the Orpheum
Voted 'Most Popular Woman Vaudeville Artist' in 1908 — before she was 23
Was carried onstage by her parents as an infant; appeared on Broadway at age six
Known for her character songs — she inhabited old maids, children, and eccentrics completely
Adapted her act carefully for early sound film, making her screen debut in 1929

name: May Robson
knownFor: An Australian-born actress with a 58-year career who earned an Academy Award nomination at age 75 for Lady for a Day (1933) — the earliest-born Oscar nominee in history at the time.
quickFacts: Born in Melbourne, Australia, 1858; died 1942
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 324) on February 5, 1924
Received an Academy Award nomination for Best Actress for Lady for a Day (1933) at age 75
The earliest-born person ever to receive an Oscar nomination at the time
Did not begin her Hollywood film career until 1927, in her late 60s
Remained top-billed in films into the late 1930s — well into her 70s

name: Dave Rubinoff
knownFor: A Russian-born concert violinist who became one of America's most famous radio personalities — 'Rubinoff and His Violin' — playing opposite Eddie Cantor on the most popular radio show of the 1930s.
quickFacts: Born in Russia, 1897; died 1986 — lived to 89
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 140) at the Grande Theatre on June 20, 1928
Became famous on The Chase and Sanborn Hour with Eddie Cantor in the 1930s
Performed at the White House for multiple presidents during the 1940s
A 1937 breach-of-promise lawsuit involving him helped popularize the phrase 'Come up and see my etchings'
Performed his famous Stradivarius in concert halls and dance halls with equal ease

name: El Brendel
knownFor: The king of the Swedish dialect act — famous for 'Yee vizz!' and 'Yumpin' yiminy!' — who was reportedly the most popular comedian in America at the dawn of talking pictures.
quickFacts: Born Elmer Goodfellow Brendel in Philadelphia, 1891; died 1964
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 281) from Philadelphia
Originally performed a German dialect act but switched to Swedish after anti-German sentiment spiked post-Lusitania
Appeared in the 1927 Oscar-winning film Wings and became a sound-film star at Fox
Was reportedly 'the most popular comedian in America' at the dawn of the talkies
His catchphrases 'Yee vizz!' and 'Yumpin' yiminy!' were known to every American in the early 1930s

name: Joe Frisco
knownFor: One of the first jazz dancers in vaudeville, famous for his stutter-inflected comedy — and so associated with San Francisco that he used the city's old nickname as his stage name.
quickFacts: Born Louis Wilson Joseph Frisco in Milan, Illinois, 1889; died 1958
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 168) on June 10, 1927 with a checkmark
Performed with the Original Dixieland Jass Band in Chicago and New York in the late 1910s — at the birth of jazz performance
F. Scott Fitzgerald referenced his iconic dance style in The Great Gatsby (1925)
His signature moves included shuffles and camel walks performed with a derby hat and cigar
His famous stutter produced some of vaudeville's most quoted one-liners

name: Gus Edwards
knownFor: The man who discovered Eddie Cantor, Groucho Marx, Walter Winchell, Eleanor Powell, and Ray Bolger — and who wrote 'School Days' and 'By the Light of the Silvery Moon.' The Aladdin hosted a party in his honor in August 1924.
quickFacts: Born Gustav Simon in Hohensalza, Germany, 1879; died 1945
The Aladdin hosted a party for Gus Edwards on August 29, 1924 (pg. 387) — the entire cast signed
As a boy he worked as a song plugger, faking spontaneous audience sing-alongs to promote sheet music
Wrote 'School Days' (1907), 'By the Light of the Silvery Moon,' and 'In My Merry Oldsmobile'
His School Days revues were talent factories: he discovered Cantor, Marx, Winchell, Powell, and Bolger
Bing Crosby played him in the 1939 biographical film The Star Maker

name: Karyl Norman
knownFor: 'The Creole Fashion Plate' — a celebrated female impersonator and quick-change artist whose glamorous Orpheum Circuit performances were legendary in vaudeville, and whose mother traveled with him to design all his elaborate gowns.
quickFacts: Born George Peduzzi in Baltimore, 1897; died 1947
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 368) with a checkmark at the Orpheum
Known for his rapid costume changes and sudden shifts from feminine elegance to lusty masculine voice
His mother traveled with him on tour and designed all his elaborate gowns
In 1930 helped establish New York's 'Pansy Craze' — a drag performance movement in nightclubs
One of the most famous female impersonators in America during the 1920s

name: Donald Brian
knownFor: The original Broadway Prince Danilo in The Merry Widow (1907) — who began singing in a Boston machine shop and ended up in over 20 Broadway musicals as one of the era's great leading tenors.
quickFacts: Born in St. John's, Newfoundland, Canada, 1877; died 1948
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 355) appearing with 'Just Married Co.'
Began his career singing with a vocal quartet while working at a Boston machine shop at age 16
Originated the role of Prince Danilo in The Merry Widow on Broadway (1907) — a landmark production
Appeared in The Girl from Utah (1914) — the show where a young Fred Astaire made his Broadway debut dancing alongside him
Made one of the early sound film experiments in 1925 using the DeForest Phonofilm sound-on-film process

name: Harriet Hoctor
knownFor: A renowned ballerina who danced en pointe up and down a London escalator, had a bronze sculpted of her by Jacob Epstein, and had George Gershwin compose 'Hoctor's Ballet' specifically for her — then danced with Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers in Shall We Dance (1937).
quickFacts: Born in Hoosick Falls, New York, 1905; died 1977
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 249) with a checkmark from New York, N.Y.
Sculptor Jacob Epstein created a bronze portrait of her during her London run (1931-32)
Famously danced en pointe up and down an escalator during her London engagement
George Gershwin composed 'Hoctor's Ballet' specifically for her in 1929
Appeared with Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers in Shall We Dance (1937)

name: Paul Althouse
knownFor: A Metropolitan Opera tenor who had a remarkable double career — first as a lyric tenor, then after retraining as a Wagnerian Heldentenor — and who later taught some of opera's greatest voices including Richard Tucker.
quickFacts: Born in Reading, Pennsylvania, 1889; died 1954
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 320) on September 3, 1924 from New York City
Performed with the Metropolitan Opera from 1913 to 1940 — nearly three decades
Made a comeback to opera in San Francisco in 1925 as Faust, which inspired him to retrain as a Heldentenor
Later taught Richard Tucker, Eleanor Steber, and Astrid Varnay — three of opera's greatest mid-century voices
One of the few singers to successfully transition from lyric to dramatic tenor roles

name: Tsuru Aoki
knownFor: Likely the first Asian actress to receive top billing in American films — a pioneering Japanese silent film star who signed the Aladdin guestbook as 'Tsuru Hayakawa,' the name of her famous husband.
quickFacts: Born in Tokyo, Japan, 1892; died 1961
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 194) on December 15, 1926 as 'Tsuru Hayakawa'
Arrived in California in 1899 with a theatrical troupe and performed at the Palace Hotel in San Francisco
Married the famous Japanese-American actor Sessue Hayakawa
Appeared in approximately 40 American films in the 1910s and made over 20 films with her husband
Likely the first Asian actress to receive top billing in American films — a genuine pioneer

name: Jimmy Savo
knownFor: A beloved mime, juggler, and clown billed as 'The Child Wonder Juggler' at age 12 — who originated a role in The Boys from Syracuse on Broadway, and whose spirit was so indomitable he performed again after a leg amputation.
quickFacts: Born in New York City, 1892; died 1960
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 312) from New York
Was billed as 'The Child Wonder Juggler' at age 12 — his career began in the streets of New York
Originated the role of Dromio of Syracuse in the 1938 Broadway production of The Boys from Syracuse
After a leg amputation in 1946 he remarkably continued performing in nightclubs, including at the Plaza Hotel
His silent pantomime was described as 'a whole story told without a word'

name: Genevieve Tobin
knownFor: A Broadway and Hollywood actress who introduced a Cole Porter song in Fifty Million Frenchmen (1929), appeared opposite Cary Grant and Humphrey Bogart in 1930s films, and lived to 95 — retiring happily after marrying director William Keighley.
quickFacts: Born in New York City, 1901; died 1995 — she lived to 95
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 363) with her sisters Vivian and Genevius Tobin — all three with checkmarks
Introduced a Cole Porter song in Fifty Million Frenchmen (1929), which relaunched her Hollywood career
Appeared opposite Cary Grant, Barbara Stanwyck, and Humphrey Bogart in 1930s films
Retired from acting in 1938 after marrying director William Keighley
Her final film was No Time for Comedy (1940) with James Stewart

name: Lew Brice
knownFor: Fanny Brice's brother and a vaudeville performer in his own right — who signed the Aladdin guestbook on the exact same page as Houdini, listed as 'Lew Brice — NYC USA.'
quickFacts: Born 1893; died 1966 — Fanny Brice's brother
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 231) directly above Harry Houdini's bold signature
Helped launch Fanny's career by convincing producers to hire her as a replacement lead in 1909
Performed in vaudeville throughout the 1920s, sometimes partnering with his wife Muriel Worth
Was briefly married to actress Mae Clarke — of the famous grapefruit scene in The Public Enemy (1931)
The Brice family was one of vaudeville's great dynasties — Fanny its most famous star

name: Herb Rawlinson
knownFor: An English-born Hollywood leading man with a 42-year career and over 100 films — who sailed to America on the same ship as Charlie Chaplin, and whose Hollywood Walk of Fame star was awarded posthumously.
quickFacts: Born in Brighton, England, 1885; died 1953
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 150) with a checkmark — marked as notable by Hattie or Minnie
Sailed to America on the same ship as Charlie Chaplin — two legends on one boat
Appeared in over 100 films including work with Tod Browning, Boris Karloff, and Humphrey Bogart
Received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame posthumously in 1960
Had a 42-year career that stretched from silent films to early television

name: Jean Hersholt
knownFor: A Danish-born character actor and great humanitarian who gave so much to the film industry's relief fund that the Academy Award for humanitarian service was named after him.
quickFacts: Born in Copenhagen, Denmark, 1876; emigrated to the US in 1913
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 261 (Hollywood) and pg. 317
On pg. 317 he signed 'Jean Hersholt — Stella-Dallas Friend' with a checkmark
Co-founded the Motion Picture Relief Fund in 1921 — the year of his first Aladdin visit
The Jean Hersholt Humanitarian Award (Academy Award) was created in his honor in 1956
Also played Dr. Christian in a long-running radio series (1937-1956)

name: Nazimova
knownFor: The great Russian-born stage and screen actress who brought a fierce, experimental intelligence to silent film and became one of Hollywood's earliest gay icons — whose apartment complex 'The Garden of Allah' was a celebrity salon.
quickFacts: Born Alla Nazimova in Yalta, Crimea, 1879; trained under Stanislavski in Moscow
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 247) as 'Nazimova — Los Angeles'
Produced and starred in Salome (1923), an art film so ahead of its time it baffled audiences
Her apartment complex in West Hollywood was known as 'The Garden of Allah' — a celebrity gathering place
Never legally married; her relationships with women were an open secret in Hollywood
Died in 1945 after a career spanning five decades

name: Julian Eltinge
knownFor: America's most famous female impersonator of the early 20th century — a vaudeville superstar who performed as women so convincingly that cosmetics companies sponsored him, and who had his own Broadway theatre.
quickFacts: Born William Dalton in Newtonville, Massachusetts, 1881
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 253) with a checkmark
At the height of his fame, the Eltinge Theatre on Broadway was named after him
His 'Julian Eltinge Magazine' sold beauty tips — the secrets of his feminine transformation
Careful to maintain a macho off-stage persona — often demonstrating his boxing skills to reporters
His career collapsed when female impersonation was criminalized in many cities in the 1930s

name: Nancy Carroll
knownFor: A red-haired Irish-American actress who became one of Paramount's biggest stars in the early talkie era, earning an Academy Award nomination for The Devil's Holiday (1930).
quickFacts: Born Ann Veronica La Hiff in New York City, 1903
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 186) on February 16, 1927 while appearing in 'Loose Ankles'
Was one of Paramount's biggest box-office draws from 1929-1932
Received an Academy Award nomination for Best Actress for The Devil's Holiday (1930)
Known for her natural acting style and appealing vulnerability on screen
Her career faded in the mid-1930s as her studio relationships soured

name: Clara Kimball Young
knownFor: One of the most popular actresses in early American silent film who signed the Aladdin guestbook on the same page as Roy Rogers and Merna Kennedy — a page crowded with the circus, the vaudeville road, and the movies.
quickFacts: Born in Chicago, 1890, to a theatrical family
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 132 (September 4, 1928) and pg. 356 (June 12, 1925)
Was among the highest-paid actresses in Hollywood between 1914-1918
At one point had her own production company, Clara Kimball Young Film Corporation
On the same September 4, 1928 page as Roy Rogers ('Anywhere') and Merna Kennedy ('The Circus')
Continued acting in supporting roles into the 1940s

name: Mischa Auer
knownFor: A Russian-born character actor whose manic, wild-eyed comic performances made him one of Hollywood's most beloved scene-stealers — and who signed the Aladdin guestbook at the very start of his American career.
quickFacts: Born Mischa Ounskowsky in St. Petersburg, Russia, 1905
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 190) on January 17, 1927 with the Bertha Kalich Company
Was only about 21 years old when he visited the Aladdin — just beginning his career
Received an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actor for My Man Godfrey (1936)
Known for his elastic face, comic timing, and memorable supporting roles
Fled Russia after the Revolution; emigrated to the US in the early 1920s

name: Floyd Bennett
knownFor: The heroic aviator who piloted Richard Byrd's plane over the North Pole in 1926 — the first men to fly over the pole — who signed the Aladdin guestbook just months later, and whose death the guestbook itself records.
quickFacts: Born in Warrensburg, New York, 1890
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 200) in November 1926 — just months after the North Pole flight
The guestbook itself notes beside his name: 'died April 25/28' — someone added this later
Won the Medal of Honor for the North Pole flight with Commander Richard Byrd in 1926
Died April 25, 1928 of pneumonia contracted while rescuing survivors of a crashed plane
Floyd Bennett Field in Brooklyn, New York is named in his honor

name: Jeanette MacDonald
knownFor: The 'Iron Butterfly' — a soprano whose voice and screen chemistry with Nelson Eddy made her one of MGM's greatest stars, who may have signed the Aladdin guestbook on New Year's Eve 1924.
quickFacts: Born in Philadelphia, 1903; began performing on Broadway as a teenager
A signature reading 'Jeanette MacD...' appears in the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 409, December 31, 1924)
Was working in Broadway shows and touring productions during this period
Her film career began in 1929; teamed with Nelson Eddy starting in 1935
Their partnership produced eight films including Naughty Marietta and Maytime
Died in 1965 — Nelson Eddy collapsed at a performance upon hearing the news

name: Belle Bennett
knownFor: A major silent film dramatic actress best known for her heart-wrenching performance in Stella Dallas (1925), who signed the Aladdin guestbook under her married name 'Mrs. Frederic E. Windamer.'
quickFacts: Born in Milwaukie, Oregon, 1891
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 341 and pg. 407 (December 16, 1924, Orpheum) as 'Mrs. Frederic E. Windamer (Belle Bennett)'
Her performance as the self-sacrificing mother in Stella Dallas (1925) is considered her masterpiece
Was a major dramatic star throughout the silent era
Died in 1932 at just 41 years old, just as the sound era was changing everything
Jean Hersholt, who also visited the Aladdin, called her one of the finest actresses he knew

name: Lloyd Corrigan
knownFor: A San Francisco native who built a long Hollywood career as both actor and director, signing the Aladdin guestbook alongside a remarkable group of theatrical colleagues all on one page.
quickFacts: Born in San Francisco, 1900 — a true San Francisco native
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 411) as 'Lloyd Corrigan — S.F. + L.A.' while in 'The Goose Hangs High'
Was on the same page as Howard Kyle, Marjorie Hayes, Jack Sheehan, and Bud Burrows
Directed films in the late 1920s and early 1930s before pivoting to a long acting career
Later played Charlie Chan's bumbling friend Tommy in the Chan film series
Appeared in over 150 films and TV shows throughout his career

name: Leslie Fenton
knownFor: A British actor and director who appeared in dozens of Hollywood films — and one of the Aladdin's most devoted guests, signing the guestbook four separate times across several years.
quickFacts: Born in Liverpool, England, 1902
Signed the Aladdin guestbook four times — pg. 378, pg. 391 (Orpheum), pg. 409 (December 31, 1924), pg. 413
On pg. 409 (New Year's Eve 1924) he signed 'Leslie Fenton — Liverpool, Eng.' with the Mitzi Company
Appeared in over 50 films including The Public Enemy (1931)
Later became a director, helming films in the 1940s including Whispering Smith (1948)
Married to actress Ann Dvorak

name: Ruth Budd
knownFor: A vaudeville and stage performer who visited the Aladdin twice and left behind the guestbook's finest one-liner: 'The morons are great!!'
quickFacts: From New York City
Signed the Aladdin guestbook twice — pg. 227 (New York) and pg. 409 (Orpheum, December 31, 1924)
On pg. 409 she added the remark 'The morons are great!!'
Was on the Orpheum Circuit — the premier vaudeville touring route
Her mother Mrs. W. Budd also signed the guestbook on pg. 227
Her name was recognized by the Mooser family as someone worth noting

name: Barrie Norton
knownFor: A New York stage performer who visited the Aladdin while touring with Little Jessie James, one of the most popular Broadway musical comedies of the early 1920s.
quickFacts: From New York City
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 397) as 'Barney Norton — N.Y.C.' with the Little Jessie James company
Little Jessie James was a popular 1923 Broadway musical comedy that toured nationally
Was part of the extensive touring circuit that brought Broadway shows to San Francisco
Other Little Jessie James cast members also signed on the same page
One of many performers whose names the Mooser family recognized from the circuits

name: May Duncan
knownFor: A performer on the Orpheum Circuit who appeared at a party thrown by Leo Carrillo and Harry Carroll at the Aladdin on March 25, 1926.
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 396) at a party hosted by Leo Carrillo and Harry Carroll
The page header reads: 'Leo Carrillo & Harry Carroll Party — March 25, 1926'
Was on the Orpheum Theatre circuit
Her entry appears alongside Leon Varona and Harry Carroll's own signature
Must have been a lively evening — Leo Carrillo was known for his outgoing personality
Not to be confused with actress Mary Duncan, who also signed the guestbook (pg. 303)

name: Howard Kyle
knownFor: A stage and screen actor who signed the Aladdin guestbook alongside Lloyd Corrigan, Marjorie Hayes, Jack Sheehan, and Bud Burrows — a page crowded with theatrical talent at the Orpheum.
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 411) at the Orpheum Theatre
His entry appears on the same page as Lloyd Corrigan, Marjorie Hayes, Jack Sheehan, and Bud Burrows
They were all associated with 'The Goose Hangs High' production
Had a career in both stage and early film work
Appeared in numerous stage productions on the national touring circuit
Part of a remarkable page that captured a whole company of performers in one visit

name: Marjorie Hayes
knownFor: An actress on the Orpheum Circuit who appears in the Aladdin guestbook on a remarkable page alongside Lloyd Corrigan, Howard Kyle, Jack Sheehan, and Bud Burrows.
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 411) at the Orpheum Theatre
Her entry is on the same page as Lloyd Corrigan, Howard Kyle, Jack Sheehan, and Bud Burrows
Was part of the active theatrical touring circuit in San Francisco in the 1920s
The name 'Maggie Hayes' in the family's records may refer to the same person
Was associated with 'The Goose Hangs High' production at the Orpheum
Part of a remarkable page that captured a whole company of performers in one visit

name: Jack Sheehan
knownFor: A performer at the Orpheum Theatre who received a checkmark in the Aladdin guestbook — Hattie or Minnie's mark for someone of note — on a page shared with Lloyd Corrigan and company.
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 411) at the Orpheum Theatre — with a checkmark (✓) next to his name
The checkmark indicates Hattie or Minnie Mooser marked him as a person worth noting
His entry is on the same page as Lloyd Corrigan, Howard Kyle, Marjorie Hayes, and Bud Burrows
Was part of the theatrical circuit that made San Francisco a regular stop
His name appears in family records as 'Jack Sheelam' — likely a transcription variant
Mrs. John Sheehan also signed the guestbook separately (pg. 389)

name: Bud Burrows
knownFor: A crowd-pleasing entertainer who visited the Aladdin so often he signed the guestbook three times — always with a comic remark, once declaring himself simply 'Hungry — all the time.'
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook three times — pg. 368 ('Hungry — all the time'), pg. 387 (Gus Edwards party), pg. 411 ('Ol' Bud Burrows — Od bless my soul!')
On pg. 368 he appears alongside Karyl Norman and Adrian Morris
On pg. 411 he appears with Lloyd Corrigan, Howard Kyle, Marjorie Hayes, and Jack Sheehan
Also signed as 'Billy Burross' and 'Billy Burress' on other pages
His humor comes through in every entry — always the comic remark
Was clearly a favorite of Hattie and Minnie — a true Aladdin regular

name: George Sterling
knownFor: San Francisco's unofficial poet laureate of the early 20th century — a romantic, bohemian figure who presided over the city's literary scene from the Bohemian Club and mentored Jack London and Ambrose Bierce.
quickFacts: Born in Sag Harbor, New York, 1869; moved to San Francisco and became the city's most celebrated poet
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 418) as 'George Sterling — S.F.'
Was the center of San Francisco's artistic community — his nickname was 'The King of Bohemia'
Mentored Jack London, Ambrose Bierce, and many other writers
His poem 'The City by the Sea' about San Francisco made him famous
Died by suicide in 1926 in his room at the Bohemian Club — the same year as Rudolph Valentino

name: Charlie Ruggles
knownFor: A beloved comic character actor who played the henpecked, mild-mannered husband to perfection in dozens of Hollywood comedies — and who visited the Aladdin with a checkmark next to his name.
quickFacts: Born in Los Angeles, 1886
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 327) as 'Chas. Ruggles — N.Y.' on July 15, 1925 with a checkmark (✓)
Was part of the Passing Show touring company when he visited
Appeared in over 100 films including Ruggles of Red Gap (1935) and Charley's Aunt (1941)
Known for his perpetually flustered, gentle comic persona
His brother Wesley Ruggles was a prominent Hollywood director

name: Leon Errol
knownFor: A rubber-legged Australian-born comedian who was one of the biggest stars of Broadway and vaudeville before conquering short-film comedies in Hollywood.
quickFacts: Born in Sydney, Australia, 1881; emigrated to the US where his career flourished
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 345) on March 30, 1924 from New York City
Famous for his unique 'drunk' routine — rubber legs that could bend in impossible directions
Was a major star in Ziegfeld Follies on Broadway before moving to films
Appeared in over 100 short films and feature films in Hollywood
Died in 1951, still performing

name: Calvin Coolidge Jr.
knownFor: The son of Calvin Coolidge — who signed the Aladdin guestbook in October 1922 when his father was Vice President, and whose death two years later at age 16 devastated the President.
quickFacts: Born 1908; signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 169) in October 1922 from Northampton, Mass.
His brother John Coolidge also signed the same page
Calvin Jr. died in July 1924 of a blood infection — just two years after signing the guestbook
His father Calvin Coolidge Sr. became President in August 1923 when Harding died
President Coolidge later wrote 'the power and glory of the Presidency went with him' about his son's death
He was 13 or 14 when he signed the guestbook — still a child visiting San Francisco

name: Mary Duncan
knownFor: A luminous actress who was one of Fox Film Corporation's major dramatic stars in the late silent and early sound era, appearing in the F.W. Murnau masterpiece City Girl (1930).
quickFacts: Born in Luttrellsville, Pennsylvania, 1895
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 303) as 'Mary Duncan — New York'
Appeared in F.W. Murnau's masterpiece City Girl (1930) — a career-defining role
Was a major Fox Film star in the late 1920s and early 1930s
Her career faded quickly with the full transition to sound films
Not to be confused with May Duncan, a different performer who also signed the guestbook (pg. 396)

name: Bertha Kalich
knownFor: A celebrated Yiddish theatre actress who crossed over to Broadway and became one of the great dramatic stage stars of the early 20th century — the 'Yiddish Duse.'
quickFacts: Born in Podgórze, Poland (then Austro-Hungarian Empire), 1874
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 190) on January 17, 1927 — heading the Bertha Kalich Company
Her entire company signed with her, including the young Mischa Auer, who was just starting out
Was a legend of the Yiddish stage before becoming a Broadway star
Known for emotionally intense dramatic performances — sometimes called 'the Yiddish Duse'
Died in 1939 after a long and distinguished career

name: Roy Rogers
knownFor: A vaudeville entertainer who signed the Aladdin guestbook on September 4, 1928 — on the exact same page as Merna Kennedy (from The Circus), Chaz Chase, and Clara Kimball Young — listing his home as simply 'Anywhere.'
quickFacts: Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 132) on September 4, 1928 — location given as 'Anywhere'
His entry appears alongside Merna Kennedy ('The Circus'), Chaz Chase (Warfield), and Clara Kimball Young
The theatrical crowd on his page confirms he was a working entertainer on the circuit
The famous cowboy Roy Rogers (Leonard Slye) adopted that stage name in 1938 — this is a different Roy Rogers
The breezy 'Anywhere' for his location is perfect — the answer of a man who lives on the road
One of many performers whose real names and stories remain to be discovered

name: Norma Talmadge
knownFor: One of the biggest box office stars in Hollywood during the silent film era — a tragic heroine who commanded her own studio, her own production company, and her own brand of fame before sound ended her career.
quickFacts: Born in Jersey City, 1894; began in films as early as 1910 at Vitagraph
Married Joseph Schenck in 1916, who became her producer and made her a major star
Ran the Norma Talmadge Film Corporation with her sister Constance and mother Peg
Her 1921 film 'The Sign on the Door' was a massive hit; she was paid $10,000 per week
When sound came, her Brooklyn accent proved fatal — she retired in 1930
Signed the Aladdin guestbook (pg. 14) with her New York address

name: Charmian London
knownFor: Wife and biographer of Jack London; a celebrated adventurer and author in her own right who managed the Jack London ranch and his literary estate after his death in 1916.
quickFacts: Married Jack London in 1905
Published The Book of Jack London (1921)
Sailed the South Seas with Jack aboard the Snark

name: Dinty Moore
knownFor: Beloved vaudeville comedian and entertainer known as "The Original Dinty Moore," whose name became so synonymous with jovial Irish-American character that it was later adopted by the famous beef stew brand.
quickFacts: Popular vaudeville and burlesque performer
His name lived on in Dinty Moore beef stew

name: Angelo J. Rossi
knownFor: Mayor of San Francisco from 1931 to 1944 — one of the longest-serving mayors in the city's history. A florist by trade who rose through civic organizations to become a beloved city leader during the Depression era.
quickFacts: Mayor of San Francisco 1931–1944
Born in Volpedo, Italy; immigrated as a child
Oversaw completion of Golden Gate Bridge groundbreaking preparations

name: Vicki Baum
knownFor: Austrian-born author whose novel Grand Hotel (1929) became a worldwide sensation, spawning the Oscar-winning 1932 MGM film starring Greta Garbo and John Barrymore. She later settled in Hollywood as a screenwriter.
quickFacts: Wrote Grand Hotel (Menschen im Hotel, 1929)
Novel adapted into Oscar-winning 1932 film with Greta Garbo
Fled Nazi Germany; became a US citizen

name: James Rolph Jr.
knownFor: Known as "Sunny Jim," he served as Mayor of San Francisco for nineteen years (1912–1931) before becoming Governor of California. Enormously popular, he was famous for his cowboy boots, top hat, and ever-present carnation.
quickFacts: Mayor of San Francisco 1912–1931
Governor of California 1931–1934
Oversaw the Panama-Pacific International Exposition (1915)

name: Yehudi Menuhin
knownFor: Child prodigy violinist who debuted with the San Francisco Symphony at age seven and astonished the world with his virtuosity. He grew up to become one of the twentieth century's most celebrated musicians, later founding his own music school in England.
quickFacts: Debuted with SF Symphony at age 7 (1923)
Studied under Louis Persinger in San Francisco
Later Baron Menuhin of Stoke d'Abernon

name: Bela Lugosi
knownFor: Hungarian-born actor who became immortal as Count Dracula in the 1931 Universal Pictures film — a role he had originated on Broadway in 1927. His aristocratic bearing and hypnotic accent made him the definitive screen vampire.
quickFacts: Played Dracula on Broadway (1927) and in film (1931)
Born Béla Ferenc Dezső Blaskó in Hungary
Became an American citizen in 1931

name: Tom Moore
knownFor: Irish-born silent film star and one of Hollywood's most popular leading men of the 1910s and 1920s. Brother of actors Matt Moore and Owen Moore; Owen was briefly married to Mary Pickford.
quickFacts: One of the famous "Three Moore Brothers" of silent film
Starred in over 100 silent films
Brother-in-law (briefly) of Mary Pickford

name: Jackie Fields
knownFor: San Francisco-born boxing champion who won Olympic gold at the 1924 Paris Games as a featherweight, then went on to become World Welterweight Champion twice (1929 and 1932). Born Jacob Finkelstein, he was one of boxing's great Jewish champions.
quickFacts: Olympic gold medalist, Paris 1924
World Welterweight Champion (1929, 1932)
Born Jacob Finkelstein in San Francisco

name: Peggy Hopkins Joyce
knownFor: The most talked-about woman in America during the 1920s — showgirl, Ziegfeld Follies star, and serial bride who married six millionaires and inspired countless gossip columns. Charlie Chaplin based the gold-digging lead of City Lights partly on her.
quickFacts: Starred in Ziegfeld Follies
Married six times, always to wealthy men
Inspired characters in Chaplin's City Lights and other films

name: Oliver Morosco
knownFor: One of the most powerful theatrical producers of the early twentieth century, with theaters bearing his name in Los Angeles and San Francisco. He launched the careers of countless stars and produced hundreds of Broadway and West Coast productions.
quickFacts: Owned the Morosco Theatre in Los Angeles and San Francisco
Produced over 200 plays
Discovered and launched many major stage careers

name: Skeet Gallagher
knownFor: Rubber-limbed Broadway and vaudeville comedian famous for his eccentric dancing and physical comedy. A favorite on the Keith circuit, he appeared in early talking pictures and was known for his loose-jointed, acrobatic comic style.
quickFacts: Broadway star of the 1920s
Known for eccentric dance comedy
Appeared in early sound films

name: Ben Blue
knownFor: Canadian-born comedian and pantomimist whose sad-faced, slow-burn style made him a vaudeville headliner and later a beloved character actor in Hollywood films of the 1930s and 1940s.
quickFacts: Vaudeville headliner across North America
Known for sad-faced pantomime comedy
Later appeared in dozens of Hollywood films

name: Warren G. Harding
knownFor: The 29th President of the United States, who died in San Francisco on August 2, 1923, while returning from a trip to Alaska — making his visit to the Aladdin one of his final social engagements in the city where he would draw his last breath.
quickFacts: 29th President of the United States (1921–1923)
Died at the Palace Hotel, San Francisco, August 2, 1923
His presidency was later shadowed by the Teapot Dome scandal

name: D.W. Griffith
knownFor: The father of cinematic language — director of The Birth of a Nation (1915) and Intolerance (1916) — who invented or perfected nearly every technique of modern filmmaking: the close-up, the tracking shot, parallel editing, and the fade. One of the most influential and controversial figures in film history.
quickFacts: Directed The Birth of a Nation (1915) and Intolerance (1916)
Pioneered the close-up, cross-cutting, and fade techniques
Co-founded United Artists with Chaplin, Pickford, and Fairbanks

name: Fanny Brice
knownFor: The queen of the Ziegfeld Follies — comedian, singer, and actress whose comedic genius and heartfelt torch songs made her one of the most beloved entertainers in America. Her Baby Snooks character ran on radio for decades, and her life inspired the musical Funny Girl.
quickFacts: Star of the Ziegfeld Follies for over two decades
Created the Baby Snooks radio character
Her life story became the musical Funny Girl (1964), played by Barbra Streisand

name: Bert Wheeler
knownFor: Half of the beloved comedy team Wheeler and Woolsey, who became RKO's biggest stars of the early sound era with a string of madcap musical comedies. Wheeler was the boyish, romantic half of the duo, with rubber-faced expressions and impeccable comic timing.
quickFacts: Teamed with Robert Woolsey from 1927 onward
Wheeler and Woolsey made over 20 films for RKO
Started in vaudeville and Broadway before pictures

name: Edward Everett Horton
knownFor: The master of the double-take and the flustered everyman — one of Hollywood's most beloved character actors, who appeared in over 150 films. He was Fred Astaire's nervous foil in Top Hat and The Gay Divorcee, and later narrated the Fractured Fairy Tales segments of the Rocky and Bullwinkle Show.
quickFacts: Appeared in over 150 films across five decades
Foil to Fred Astaire in Top Hat (1935) and The Gay Divorcee (1934)
Later voiced Fractured Fairy Tales on The Rocky & Bullwinkle Show

name: Jason Robards Sr.
knownFor: A prolific silent film and early talking-picture actor who appeared in dozens of serials and features in the 1920s. Father of Jason Robards Jr., who would become one of America's greatest stage and screen actors, winning two Academy Awards.
quickFacts: Appeared in over 100 films in the 1920s–30s
Father of Oscar-winning actor Jason Robards Jr.
Known for serials and adventure pictures

name: Mike Donlin
knownFor: Known as "Turkey Mike," one of the great outfielders of the Deadball Era who batted .333 lifetime and starred for the New York Giants. After baseball he pursued an acting career in vaudeville and silent films, making him one of the first crossover athlete-entertainers.
quickFacts: .333 career batting average
Starred for the New York Giants (1904–1908)
Married actress Mabel Hite; pursued vaudeville and film career after baseball

name: Paul Ash
knownFor: The most flamboyant theater bandleader of the 1920s, who packed houses at the Oriental Theatre in Chicago and the Mark Strand in New York with his charismatic conducting style, outrageous costumes, and crowd-pleasing showmanship. Called "The Mayor of State Street" by his Chicago fans.
quickFacts: Resident bandleader at Chicago's Oriental Theatre in the 1920s
Known as "The Mayor of State Street"
Pioneer of the theatrical big-band presentation style

name: Rex Cherryman
knownFor: A handsome and critically acclaimed Broadway leading man of the 1920s who died tragically of septicemia aboard a ship at age 30 in 1928, cutting short a brilliant career. He was engaged to actress Fay Wray at the time of his death.
quickFacts: Leading Broadway actor of the 1920s
Engaged to Fay Wray (future star of King Kong) at his death
Died at sea of blood poisoning, age 30 (1928)

name: Bill Desmond
knownFor: A Canadian-born action star and serial king of silent cinema who thrilled audiences in dozens of adventure serials throughout the 1920s. Rugged and athletic, he was one of the most reliable and popular leading men of the action genre.
quickFacts: Starred in numerous silent film serials of the 1920s
Known as a reliable action and adventure leading man
Appeared in over 150 films throughout his career

name: Jim Tully
knownFor: Known as "the Gorky of the Gutter," Tully was one of the most celebrated and controversial writers of the 1920s. A former hobo, circus hand, and prize-fighter, he wrote raw autobiographical novels including Beggars of Life (1924) — later made into a film by William Wellman — and was a fixture of Hollywood literary circles. He signed in as "Jiggs," the lovable working-class hero of the Bringing Up Father comic strip.
quickFacts: Former hobo and circus hand turned celebrated author
Wrote Beggars of Life (1924), filmed by William Wellman
Called "the Gorky of the Gutter" by H.L. Mencken

name: Mrs. William Ritter
knownFor: Wife of marine biologist Dr. William Emerson Ritter, founder of the Scripps Institution of Oceanography in La Jolla. She signed the guestbook as "Maggie" — the sharp-tongued, socially ambitious wife from the beloved comic strip Bringing Up Father, the perfect foil to her companion Jim Tully's "Jiggs."
quickFacts: Wife of Dr. William Emerson Ritter, founder of Scripps Institution of Oceanography
Signed as "Maggie" to match Jim Tully's "Jiggs" — the feuding couple from Bringing Up Father
Bringing Up Father ran in newspapers from 1913 to 2000

name: Christine Hart
knownFor: San Francisco resident who signed at 1806 Leavenworth — a guest of the Aladdin in its early years.

name: Mrs. M. Dennison Robinson
knownFor: A guest of the Aladdin from the Country Club Apartments in Venice — part of the fashionable California social set who frequented the Tiffin Room in its early years.

name: Mrs. Ernest J. Mott
knownFor: San Francisco resident at 2808 Vallejo Street — one of the city's most prestigious addresses, in the heart of Pacific Heights — who signed the Aladdin guestbook alongside the celebrated actress Norma Talmadge.

name: Mrs. Edward T. Rainey
knownFor: San Francisco resident at 2715 Steiner Street, Pacific Heights, who signed alongside Norma Talmadge — one of the most famous actresses in the world at the time — in the Aladdin's early guestbook.

name: E. de Quiñónez
knownFor: A visitor from San Salvador, Central America — likely a diplomat or distinguished citizen of El Salvador — who signed the Aladdin guestbook, reflecting the cosmopolitan reach of Hattie and Minnie's salon.
quickFacts: Signed from San Salvador, C.A.

name: Reginald Travers
knownFor: San Francisco guest who signed the Aladdin guestbook in the club's first year of operation.

name: Sydney Schlesinger
knownFor: A recurring guest who appears multiple times in the Aladdin guestbook — a loyal friend of the Tiffin Room who kept coming back throughout the 1920s.
